title: Opioid Overdose
targetAudience: Medical Students, Residents, Emergency Medicine, Toxicology, Addiction Medicine

vignette.text:
EMS brings a 29-year-old man found unresponsive with agonal breathing. Bystanders report known heroin use. He received 2mg intranasal naloxone en route with minimal response.

Past Medical History: Opioid use disorder

Medications: Unknown

Allergies: Unknown

physicalExam.lungs: Decreased breath sounds bilaterally, coarse rhonchi

questions.stem: This patient has unresponsiveness, respiratory depression, and pinpoint pupils.
questions.question: What is the classic opioid toxidrome?

options.text: Mydriasis is characteristic
Hyperthermia is expected
Tachycardia is typical

OPIOID TOXIDROME TRIAD: (1) CNS DEPRESSION: Decreased level of consciousness; Sedation → unresponsive; (2) RESPIRATORY DEPRESSION: Decreased respiratory rate (<12/min); Hypoxemia; Apnea in severe cases; (3) MIOSIS: Pinpoint pupils; May be absent with: Meperidine, tramadol, hypoxia, co-ingestion; ADDITIONAL FINDINGS: Hypotension; Bradycardia; Hypothermia; Decreased bowel sounds; Track marks (IV use); COMPLICATIONS: Aspiration pneumonia; Non-cardiogenic pulmonary edema; Rhabdomyolysis (prolonged immobilization); Compartment syndrome; Hypoxic brain injury; FENTANYL-SPECIFIC: Rapid onset; Chest wall rigidity (wooden chest); May require higher naloxone doses

questions.explanation: Opioid Toxidrome Triad: CNS DEPRESSION (sedation to unresponsive), RESPIRATORY DEPRESSION (<12/min, hypoxemia, apnea), MIOSIS (pinpoint pupils - may be absent with meperidine, tramadol, hypoxia). ADDITIONAL: Hypotension, bradycardia, hypothermia. COMPLICATIONS: Aspiration, pulmonary edema, rhabdomyolysis, hypoxic brain injury. FENTANYL: Rapid onset, chest wall rigidity, may need higher naloxone.

sources.text: StatPearls: Opioid Overdose. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: The patient received 2mg IN naloxone with minimal response.
questions.question: How should naloxone be administered?

options.text: NALOXONE ADMINISTRATION: ROUTES: IV (fastest): 0.04-0.4mg initial; Escalate to 2mg if no response; IM/SC: 0.4-2mg; IN: 4mg (commercial device); GOALS: Restore adequate breathing (RR >12); Maintain SpO2 >94%; DO NOT need to fully awaken patient; TITRATION STRATEGY: Start LOW to avoid precipitated withdrawal; Repeat every 2-3 minutes if no response; Can give up to 10mg total; If no response to 10mg, consider other diagnosis; SPECIAL SITUATIONS: Fentanyl: May need higher doses (>10mg reported); Long-acting opioids: May need infusion; Opioid-dependent patients: Start lower (0.04mg); PRECIPITATED WITHDRAWAL: Agitation, vomiting, diarrhea; Tachycardia, hypertension; Not life-threatening but very uncomfortable; Manage supportively
Goal is to fully awaken patient
Maximum total dose is 2mg
Always give 2mg IV as initial dose

questions.explanation: Naloxone Administration: IV fastest (0.04-0.4mg initial, escalate to 2mg), IM/SC (0.4-2mg), IN (4mg). GOALS: Restore breathing (RR >12, SpO2 >94%), NOT to fully awaken. TITRATE starting low in opioid-dependent to avoid precipitated withdrawal. Can give up to 10mg; if no response, consider other diagnosis. FENTANYL may need higher doses.

sources.text: StatPearls: Opioid Overdose. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: After 4mg IV naloxone, respirations improve to 14/min and SpO2 is 96%.
questions.question: What monitoring is required after naloxone?

options.text: POST-NALOXONE MONITORING: NALOXONE HALF-LIFE: 30-90 minutes; SHORTER than most opioids!; RECURRENCE RISK: Re-sedation can occur as naloxone wears off; Higher risk with: Long-acting opioids (methadone, extended-release); Body packing/stuffing; Large ingestions; MONITORING: Observe minimum 4-6 hours after last naloxone dose; Longer for long-acting opioids (12-24h); Watch for respiratory depression recurrence; NALOXONE INFUSION: For recurrent sedation; 2/3 of reversal dose per hour; Titrate to maintain adequate ventilation; DISCHARGE CRITERIA: Alert and oriented; Normal vital signs; RR >12 for >4 hours without naloxone; No evidence of long-acting opioid; Able to walk; Social support available
1 hour observation is sufficient
Infusion is never needed
Naloxone lasts longer than opioids

questions.explanation: Post-Naloxone Monitoring: Naloxone half-life 30-90 min, SHORTER than most opioids. RECURRENCE RISK especially with long-acting opioids, body packing. OBSERVE minimum 4-6 hours after last naloxone (longer for long-acting opioids). NALOXONE INFUSION for recurrent sedation (2/3 reversal dose per hour). DISCHARGE only if alert, normal vitals, RR >12 for >4 hours.

sources.text: StatPearls: Opioid Overdose. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: Before naloxone worked, the patient required airway support.
questions.question: What is the approach to airway management in opioid overdose?

options.text: AIRWAY MANAGEMENT: IMMEDIATE STEPS: Open airway (jaw thrust, head tilt); Bag-valve-mask ventilation; High-flow O2; Suction secretions; INDICATIONS FOR INTUBATION: No response to naloxone; Aspiration or significant pulmonary edema; Unable to protect airway despite naloxone; Concomitant condition requiring intubation; NALOXONE BEFORE INTUBATION: Try naloxone first if possible; May avoid need for intubation; Give during RSI preparation if apneic; COMPLICATIONS: Aspiration pneumonia: Common; Non-cardiogenic pulmonary edema: 'Heroin lung'; Hypoxic brain injury; CHEST WALL RIGIDITY (Fentanyl): 'Wooden chest syndrome'; Prevents adequate ventilation; May need paralytic for intubation
Bag-mask ventilation is contraindicated
Always intubate before naloxone
Chest wall rigidity is rare

questions.explanation: Airway Management: IMMEDIATE - Open airway, bag-valve-mask, high-flow O2, suction. INTUBATION if no naloxone response, aspiration, can't protect airway. TRY NALOXONE FIRST if possible. COMPLICATIONS: Aspiration pneumonia, non-cardiogenic pulmonary edema, hypoxic injury. FENTANYL: Chest wall rigidity may require paralytic for intubation.

sources.text: StatPearls: Opioid Overdose. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.question: What complications should be evaluated after opioid overdose?

options.text: No need for CXR if breathing improves
Pulmonary edema is immediate only
Rhabdomyolysis doesn't occur with opioids

OPIOID OVERDOSE COMPLICATIONS: PULMONARY: Aspiration pneumonia; Non-cardiogenic pulmonary edema (may be delayed); ARDS; NEUROLOGIC: Hypoxic brain injury; Anoxic encephalopathy; MUSCULOSKELETAL: Rhabdomyolysis (from prolonged immobilization); Compartment syndrome; Check CK, monitor UOP; INFECTIOUS: Skin/soft tissue infections; Endocarditis (IVDU); HIV, Hepatitis B/C; CO-INGESTIONS: Benzodiazepines (won't respond to naloxone); Stimulants (cocaine, meth); Alcohol; ADULTERATED SUPPLY: Xylazine ('tranq'): Causes wounds, doesn't respond to naloxone; Fentanyl analogs; WORKUP: CXR (aspiration, edema); CK (rhabdomyolysis); ECG (QT prolongation with methadone); Tox screen

questions.explanation: Overdose Complications: PULMONARY - Aspiration, non-cardiogenic pulmonary edema (may be delayed), ARDS. NEURO - Hypoxic brain injury. MSK - Rhabdomyolysis, compartment syndrome (from immobilization). INFECTIOUS - Skin infections, endocarditis. CO-INGESTIONS won't respond to naloxone. ADULTERATED supply with xylazine. WORKUP: CXR, CK, ECG, tox screen.

sources.text: StatPearls: Opioid Overdose. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: The patient recovers and wants help with addiction.
questions.question: What addiction treatment should be offered?

options.text: ADDICTION TREATMENT FROM ED: MEDICATIONS FOR OUD (MOUD): Buprenorphine: Can initiate in ED; Safe, effective, reduces mortality; No special waiver needed; Methadone: Requires OTP (can't prescribe from ED); Naltrexone: Must be opioid-free 7-10 days first; BUPRENORPHINE INDUCTION: Wait for mild-moderate withdrawal (COWS ≥8); Start 4mg SL, can repeat in 1-2h; Target 8-16mg on day 1; Provide bridge prescription + referral; HARM REDUCTION: Naloxone kit (prescribe to ALL patients); Safe use education; Needle exchange info; Fentanyl test strips; NEVER DISCHARGE: Without naloxone; Without MOUD discussion; Without follow-up plan; Into withdrawal without treatment option
MOUD is not appropriate from ED
Naloxone kits only for certain patients
Special waiver needed for buprenorphine

questions.explanation: Addiction Treatment from ED: MOUD - Buprenorphine can be initiated in ED (no special waiver needed), reduces mortality. Induct when in mild-moderate withdrawal (COWS ≥8), start 4mg, target 8-16mg day 1. HARM REDUCTION: Naloxone kit for ALL patients. NEVER DISCHARGE without naloxone, MOUD discussion, and follow-up plan.

sources.text: StatPearls: Opioid Overdose. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.question: What is the complete management approach?

options.text: No addiction treatment discussion in ED
Full awakening required before stopping naloxone

Complete approach: (1) RECOGNIZE TOXIDROME: Unresponsive + RR 6 + pinpoint pupils + SpO2 78% = opioid overdose; (2) IMMEDIATE AIRWAY: Bag-valve-mask ventilation; (3) NALOXONE: 2mg IN given, give additional 2-4mg IV; Goal: RR >12, SpO2 >94% (not full awakening); (4) RESPONSE: Now RR 14, SpO2 96% - adequate; (5) EVALUATE COMPLICATIONS: CXR (aspiration/edema); CK (rhabdomyolysis); ECG; Consider co-ingestions; (6) MONITOR: Minimum 4-6 hours for re-sedation; May need naloxone infusion; (7) ADDICTION TREATMENT: Discuss buprenorphine; Can initiate from ED when in mild withdrawal; (8) DISCHARGE WITH: Naloxone prescription/kit; MOUD referral or bridge prescription; Clear follow-up plan; (9) HARM REDUCTION education
1 hour observation then discharge

questions.explanation: Complete Approach: RECOGNIZE toxidrome. IMMEDIATE airway support. NALOXONE titrated to adequate breathing (not full awakening). EVALUATE for complications (CXR, CK, ECG). MONITOR 4-6 hours minimum. ADDICTION TREATMENT - discuss and consider initiating buprenorphine. DISCHARGE with naloxone, MOUD referral, follow-up plan, and harm reduction education.

sources.text: StatPearls: Opioid Overdose. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

references.title: AACT Position Paper: Single-Dose Activated Charcoal

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is the MOST important initial step in evaluating a patient with suspected opioid overdose?

options.text: Obtain a thorough history and physical examination
options.explanation: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in opioid overdose. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.

options.text: Order advanced imaging immediately
options.explanation: Advanced imaging may be indicated but should be guided by clinical findings, not ordered reflexively.

options.text: Start empiric treatment before evaluation
options.explanation: Treatment should generally follow an appropriate diagnostic evaluation unless the patient is critically unstable.

options.text: Consult a specialist before any workup
options.explanation: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.

mcqs.stem: A patient presents with classic features of opioid overdose. Which clinical reasoning principle is MOST important in avoiding diagnostic error?

options.text: Search satisficing - stop looking after the first diagnosis
options.explanation: Premature closure (stopping the diagnostic process too early) is a common cognitive error that leads to missed diagnoses.

options.text: Consider the differential diagnosis systematically
options.explanation: Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.

options.text: Rely primarily on pattern recognition
options.explanation: While pattern recognition (System 1 thinking) is useful, it should be balanced with analytical reasoning (System 2) to avoid cognitive biases.

options.text: Order every available test to rule out all possibilities
options.explanation: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.

mcqs.stem: When managing opioid overdose, which approach best supports evidence-based practice?

options.text: Follow institutional protocols regardless of patient factors
options.explanation: Evidence-based practice integrates best evidence with clinical expertise AND patient values/circumstances.

options.text: Base decisions solely on clinical experience
options.explanation: Clinical experience is valuable but can introduce bias; it should be combined with current evidence.

options.text: Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values
options.explanation: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.

options.text: Always defer to the most recent published study
options.explanation: Individual studies should be appraised for quality and relevance; systematic reviews and guidelines generally provide stronger evidence.

mcqs.stem: A patient presents 2 hours after ingesting an unknown quantity of acetaminophen. Serum APAP level at 4 hours post-ingestion is 220 mcg/mL. Using the Rumack-Matthew nomogram, what is the appropriate management?

options.text: Start IV N-acetylcysteine (NAC) — the level is above the treatment line
options.explanation: On the Rumack-Matthew nomogram, the treatment line at 4 hours is 150 mcg/mL (FDA line) or 200 mcg/mL (original Rumack-Matthew line). At 220 mcg/mL, the level exceeds both thresholds. IV NAC (150 mg/kg loading, then 50 mg/kg over 4h, then 100 mg/kg over 16h) should be started immediately.

options.text: Recheck level at 8 hours before deciding on treatment
options.explanation: With a clearly supratherapeutic level at 4 hours, treatment should begin immediately. Delaying NAC beyond 8 hours post-ingestion significantly increases risk of hepatotoxicity.

options.text: Administer activated charcoal only — NAC is not indicated within 4 hours of ingestion
options.explanation: Activated charcoal may be beneficial if given within 1-2 hours of ingestion, but it is NOT a substitute for NAC when the level indicates toxicity. NAC should be started as soon as possible.

options.text: Obtain LFTs and treat only if transaminases are elevated
options.explanation: Hepatotoxicity from acetaminophen is delayed (ALT typically rises at 24-72 hours). Normal LFTs at 4 hours do not exclude impending liver injury. The nomogram guides treatment BEFORE liver damage occurs.

sections.content: Systematic approach to Opioid Overdose including recognition, diagnosis, and management.

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content: Evidence-based clinical pearls for Opioid Overdose.
